figures.caption: Table 8: Effect of freezing the output layer. Sort-only [128] config, 5 simulations.
Table 9: Hybrid tensor→sort vs. pure sort-only. Test error (%).
Table 10: Cumulative error reduction (%) as components are added.

Figure 5: Permutation cones. The argsort encoding partitions Rd into d! convex cones (Weyl chambers), separated by hyperplanes {xi = xj}. Shown for d = 3: six orderings of three coordinates. All points within a cone map to the same permutation. The dotted circle illustrates the stability radius from Theorem 4: perturbations smaller than δmin(x)/2 cannot cross a boundary.
Table 16: Missing features on Digits (64 features). Error (%) at each masking level.
Table 17: Missing-feature degradation: absolute error increase (pp) from m=0% to m=50%.
Table 15: Missing features on Iris (4 features). Error (%) at each masking level.
Table 14: Privacy via rank transform: error on raw vs. ranked features. ∆ = degradation in pp.
Table 28: Energy per layer, single data point. ArrowFlow [128] (V=64, N=128) vs. MLP (64→128). All energies at 45nm CMOS.
Table 27: Energy per operation at 45nm CMOS [Horowitz, 2014]. ArrowFlow uses only operations in the top group; standard neural networks use operations in both groups.
Table 24: MNIST via PCA: test error (%). Best baseline and best ArrowFlow config per setting. ArrowFlow best = [1024,128] 3-layer, 7-view ensemble. Iterations scale with training size (100/200/400).

Figure 3: Permutation-matrix accumulation. (a) Accumulator starts as identity (encoding current filter [A,B,C,D]). (b) Training input [C,A,D,B]: each item votes for its position in the input (Def. 2). (c) Votes sum additively (Eq. 4). (d) Weighted average and argsort yield the updated filter [A,C,B,D] (Eq. 5), which has moved toward the training data. Convergence is guaranteed under Proposition 8.

Table 23: Batch effect Type 2: per-gene log-normal scaling. Error (%) on 15 genes. σ = 0 is clean; σ = 0.3 is routine batch variation; σ = 0.7 is cross-platform; σ ≥ 1.0 is severe/unrealistic. Rank-based methods degrade gracefully; raw-input methods fail early.

Table 22: Batch effect Type 1: monotone per-sample transforms applied to test data. All methods trained on clean data (15 genes). Rank-based methods (ArrowFlow native, SVMranked) are perfectly invariant; raw-input methods catastrophically fail.
Table 26: The polynomial trade-off. Each cell shows whether ArrowFlow has a structural advantage (+), disadvantage (−), or is neutral (◦) relative to baselines.
Table 25: ArrowFlow architecture scaling on MNIST (PCA=64, N=10,000). Error (%) ± SE.
Table 1: Best ArrowFlow configuration vs. GridSearchCV-tuned baselines. Test error (%), mean ± std over 5 simulations. Bold = best overall.
Table 13: Noise degradation analysis: absolute error increase (pp) from σ=0 to σ=2.0. Lower = more robust. pol deg=1 datasets highlighted.

Figure 4: Multi-view ensemble. A single preprocessed input is projected through K different matrices using diverse strategies (target-aware, random, calibrated). Each produces a different permutation—a different ordinal “view.” Independent ArrowFlow networks are trained per view; predictions are combined by majority vote. Projection diversity approximates the independence condition of Theorem 9.

Figure 2: Ranking layer forward pass. Input πx = [C,A,E,B,D] is compared to three filters. The motion mj[p] = rank(rj, πx[p])−p measures signed displacement (red = forward, blue = backward). The footrule distance Dj = ∑ |mj[p]| totals the displacements. Filters are ranked by proximity: r2 (distance 2) is closest. The output π′ becomes the input to the next layer.
Table 29: Full inference energy comparison on Digits. ArrowFlow: 7-view [256], V=64. MLP: 64→128→10. Arithmetic energy only (45nm CMOS).

Figure 1: ArrowFlow pipeline. Real-valued input x is encoded via polynomial expansion, standardization, random projection, and argsort. K independent networks receive different projections Wk. Each network (detail, right) is a stack of sort layers computing Spearman’s footrule distance to learned filters. Predictions are combined by majority vote.

Figure 6: The polynomial trade-off. At degree 1 (left), features are well-separated: large gaps yield a wide stability region, but limited capacity. At degree > 1 (right), cross-terms create small gaps: higher capacity but noise amplified by Bk−1, shrinking stability. This parameter is a master switch between robustness and accuracy.

Table 21: Gene expression cancer classification. Test error (%) on TCGA PANCAN (161 test samples). “Raw” = original expression values; “Ranked” = within-sample rank transform; “AF native” = rank-transform fed directly as permutations; “AF proj” = standard ArrowFlow projection pipeline.
Table 20: Sushi preference classification. Error (%). ArrowFlow native = rankings fed directly as permutations; ArrowFlow projection = standard pipeline.
Table 11: Noise robustness on Wine (pol deg=1). Error (%) at each noise level.
Table 12: Noise robustness on Digits (pol deg=1). Error (%) at each noise level.
Table 4: Effect of network depth on test error (%).
Table 3: Effect of filter count (width) on test error (%).

Table 2: ArrowFlow vs. kNN on the same encoded permutations (fixed config per dataset; Digits errors differ slightly from Table 1 due to different config/seed). Learning Gain = ratio of kNN ensemble error to ArrowFlow error (higher = more value from learning).
